questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.questionText: Which one of the following equations related to the motion of an object is NOT correct? (Symbols carry their usual meanings)
options: u = v − at
u2 − v2 = 2as
Distance traveled during the nth second = u + (1/2)a(2n - 1)
questions.solution:
The equation that’s NOT correct is Option 3. The standard “third” equation of motion is

$$v^2 = u^2 + 2as$$

which can be rearranged as

$$u^2 - v^2 = -\,2as$$

—not $$u^2 - v^2 = 2as\,. $$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.questionText: An object is placed between infinity and the pole (P) of a convex mirror. The position of the image is
options: between pole (P) and the focus (F), behind the mirror
between the focus (F) and infinity, behind the mirror
between the pole (P) and the infinity, in front of the mirror
at the focus (F), behind the mirror
questions.solution:
For a convex mirror the mirror‐formula

$$\frac1u + \frac1v = \frac1f$$

applies, with

$u>0$ (object in front of mirror),

$f<0$ (convex mirror focal length),

$v<0$ (image behind mirror).

If $u\to\infty$, then $1/v=1/f$ ⇒ $v=f$ (image at the focus, behind).

As $u$ decreases from ∞ toward 0, $\;1/v=1/f-1/u$ becomes more negative ⇒ $|v|$ decreases from $|f|$ toward 0.

So for any finite object distance the image lies between the pole $P$ and the focus $F$, behind the mirror.

Answer: Option 1.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.questionText: Which one of the following statements about speed and velocity is correct?
options: Speed and velocity both are vector quantities.
Speed and velocity both are scalar quantities.
Speed is vector quantity and velocity is scalar quantity.
Speed is scalar quantity and velocity is vector quantity.
questions.solution:
The correct statement about speed and velocity is:

Option D: Speed is a scalar quantity and velocity is a vector quantity.

Here's a detailed explanation:

Speed: Speed is a scalar quantity. This means it only has magnitude and no direction. Speed is the rate at which an object covers distance. For example, if a car travels 60 kilometers per hour, this value indicates how fast the car is moving but does not specify the direction.

Velocity: Velocity, on the other hand, is a vector quantity. This means it includes both magnitude and direction. Velocity is the rate at which an object changes its position. For example, a car moving 60 kilometers per hour to the north has a velocity that specifies both the speed and the direction of movement.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.questionText: Which one of the following is a non-conventional source of energy?
questions.solution:
Non-conventional (renewable or alternative) sources are things like solar, wind, tidal, geothermal, etc.

• Petroleum, natural gas and coal are all conventional (fossil) fuels.

• Tidal energy harnesses the rise and fall of ocean tides, making it a renewable—hence non-conventional—source.

Answer: Option 3 (Tidal energy).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.questionText: The presence of magnetic field can be determined using which one of the following instruments?
questions.solution:
The correct answer is Option C: Magnetic needle. Here's why:

A magnetic needle, often found in a compass, is specifically designed to align itself with the magnetic field lines of the Earth. This alignment is the basis of how compasses work.

Let's look at why the other options are incorrect:

  • Option A: Ammeter - An ammeter measures the flow of electric current, not magnetic fields.
  • Option B: Voltmeter - A voltmeter measures electrical potential difference, not magnetic fields.
  • Option D: Motor - A motor uses a magnetic field to produce rotation, but it doesn't directly measure the presence of a magnetic field.

In summary, a magnetic needle is the most direct way to detect the presence of a magnetic field because it physically responds to the field's influence.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.questionText: Which one of the following conclusions could not be derived from Rutherford's α-particle scattering experiment?
options: Most of the space in the atom is empty
The radius of the atom is about 10<sup>5</sup> times the radius of the nucleus
Electrons move in a circular path of fixed energy called orbits
Nearby all the mass of the atom resides in the nucleus
questions.solution:
Option C cannot be derived from Rutherford's $\alpha$-particle scattering experiment.

Rutherford's experiment led to several conclusions about the structure of the atom:

Option A: Most of the space in the atom is empty. This conclusion was drawn because most of the $\alpha$-particles passed through the gold foil without any deflection, indicating that atoms are mostly empty space.

Option B: The radius of the atom is about $10^5$ times the radius of the nucleus. This was inferred from the fact that very few $\alpha$-particles were deflected back, suggesting a tiny, dense nucleus compared to the overall size of the atom.

Option D: Nearly all the mass of the atom resides in the nucleus. This conclusion was reached because the deflection of some $\alpha$-particles implied a very dense center, which contains most of the atomic mass.

Option C refers to the description of electron orbits, which was developed later by Niels Bohr. Bohr's model introduced the concept of electrons in specific orbits with fixed energies, an idea not covered or implied by Rutherford's scattering experiment.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.questionText: Which one of the following statements for the work done by gravity on a body is NOT correct ?
options: Work done is independent of the path followed by the body
Work done depends only on the vertical distance separating the initial and the final position of the body
Work done depends on the path followed by the body
Work done is zero if the body is displaced horizontally to the force of gravity
questions.solution:
The incorrect statement is:

Option 3: Work done depends on the path followed by the body

Step-by-step explanation

Gravity is a conservative force. For a conservative force:

  • Work done does not depend on the path

  • It depends only on the initial and final positions

For gravity, work done is given by:

$$ W = mg(h_1 - h_2) $$

where:

  • $m$ = mass of the body

  • $g$ = acceleration due to gravity

  • $h_1$ = initial height

  • $h_2$ = final height

So:

  • If only the vertical height changes, gravity does work.

  • If the displacement is purely horizontal, then there is no change in height, so work done by gravity is zero.

Checking each option

  1. Work done is independent of the path followed by the body

    This is correct.

  2. Work done depends only on the vertical distance separating the initial and the final position of the body

    This is correct.

  3. Work done depends on the path followed by the body

    This is not correct.

  4. Work done is zero if the body is displaced horizontally to the force of gravity

    This is correct.

Final answer

$$ \boxed{\text{Option 3}} $$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.questionText: Which one of the following findings is NOT observed in Rutherford's Nuclear Model of Atom?
options: There is a neutral centre in an atom called nucleus
Nearly all the mass of an atom resides in the nucleus
The electrons revolve around the nucleus in a circular path
The size of a nucleus is very small as compared to the size of atom
questions.solution:
The statement NOT supported by Rutherford’s model is:

Option 1: “There is a neutral centre in an atom called nucleus.”

Rutherford’s gold-foil experiment showed the nucleus is extremely small, contains nearly all the atom’s mass and carries a positive charge. Electrons do revolve around it in the surrounding empty space.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.questionText: To help deep-sea divers breathe, they carry cylinders of oxygen mixed with

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.questionText: 'Beats' is a phenomenon that occurs when frequencies of two harmonic waves are
questions.solution:
The phenomenon of "beats" occurs when the frequencies of two harmonic waves are nearly the same.

When two waves of slightly different frequencies interfere with each other, they create a phenomenon known as beats. The resulting sound frequency that is heard is the average of the two frequencies, while the amplitude of the sound oscillates at the difference of the two frequencies. The beat frequency can be expressed mathematically as:

$$ f_{\text{beat}} = |f_1 - f_2| $$

where $ f_1 $ and $ f_2 $ are the frequencies of the two waves. This oscillation in amplitude leads to a pulsing effect, which is the characteristic sound associated with beats.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.questionText:
Given below are the four cases in which certain heat transfer is taking place:

1. Ice is melting in a glass full of water

2. Water is boiling in an open container

3. A metal rod is heated in a furnace

4. A cup of coffee is allowed to cool on a table

In which of the above cases, the Newton's Law of Cooling is applicable ?
questions.solution:
Newton’s law of cooling applies when a body (with uniform temperature) exchanges heat by convection (or convection + radiation) with its surroundings, without any phase change. In formula form:

$$\frac{dT}{dt}=-k\bigl(T(t)-T_\infty\bigr)$$

where

• $T(t)$ is the object’s temperature at time $t$,

• $T_\infty$ is the ambient temperature,

• $k$ is a positive constant (proportional to the heat‐transfer coefficient).

Checking each case:

Ice melting in water

– Phase change at constant $0^\circ$C → temperature doesn’t change, so the simple $dT/dt$ law doesn’t apply.

Water boiling in an open container

– Phase change at $100^\circ$C → again constant temperature during boiling.

Metal rod heated in a furnace

– Often involves conduction along the rod and radiation at high temperatures; temperature is not uniform and there’s more than simple convection.

Cup of coffee cooling on a table

– No phase change, coffee cools by convection/radiation, roughly uniform temperature → fits Newton’s law.

Answer: Only case 4.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.questionText: A 100 g sphere is moving at a speed of 20 m/s and collides with another. sphere of mass 50 g. If the second sphere was at rest prior to the collision and the first sphere comes at rest immediately after the collision, considering the collision to be elastic, the speed of the second sphere would be
questions.solution:
To determine the speed of the second sphere after an elastic collision, we can use the principle of conservation of momentum and the fact that kinetic energy is conserved in an elastic collision. Let's denote the masses and velocities of the spheres as follows:

Mass of the first sphere, $$m_1 = 100 \, \text{g} = 0.1 \, \text{kg}$$

Initial velocity of the first sphere, $$u_1 = 20 \, \text{m/s}$$

Mass of the second sphere, $$m_2 = 50 \, \text{g} = 0.05 \, \text{kg}$$

Initial velocity of the second sphere, $$u_2 = 0 \, \text{m/s}$$

Final velocity of the first sphere, $$v_1 = 0 \, \text{m/s}$$

We need to find the final velocity of the second sphere, $$v_2$$.

Using the conservation of momentum:

$$m_1 u_1 + m_2 u_2 = m_1 v_1 + m_2 v_2$$

Substituting the given values:

$$0.1 \times 20 + 0.05 \times 0 = 0.1 \times 0 + 0.05 \times v_2$$

$$2 = 0.05 \times v_2$$

Solving for $$v_2$$:

$$v_2 = \frac{2}{0.05} = 40 \, \text{m/s}$$

Therefore, the speed of the second sphere after the collision is 40 m/s. The correct answer is:

Option D

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.questionText: A vehicle starts moving along a straight line path from rest. In first t seconds it moves with an acceleration of 2 m/s<sup>2</sup> and then in next 10 seconds it moves with an acceleration of 5 m/s<sup>2</sup>. The total distance travelled by the vehicle is 550 m. The value of time t is
questions.solution:
In the first $t$ seconds (acceleration $2\ \text{m/s}^2$):

• Distance $s_1 = \tfrac12\cdot2\cdot t^2 = t^2$

• Final speed $v_1 = 2t$

In the next 10 s (acceleration $5\ \text{m/s}^2$, initial speed $v_1$):

$ s_2 = v_1\cdot10 + \tfrac12\cdot5\cdot10^2 = 2t\cdot10 + \tfrac12\cdot5\cdot100 = 20t + 250 $

Total distance is 550 m, so

$ s_1 + s_2 = t^2 + (20t + 250) = 550 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad t^2 + 20t - 300 = 0 $

Solve the quadratic:

$ t = \frac{-20 \pm \sqrt{20^2 - 4\cdot1\cdot(-300)}}{2} = \frac{-20 \pm \sqrt{1600}}{2} = \frac{-20 \pm 40}{2} $

The positive root is $t = 10$ s.

Answer: 10 s.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.questionText: When a light ray passes through from air to water with a non-zero angle the ray will be
questions.solution:
When light goes from air (n₁≈1.00) into water (n₂≈1.33) at some angle θ₁ to the normal, Snell’s law tells us:

$$n_1\sin\theta_1 \;=\; n_2\sin\theta_2\,. $$

Because n₂>n₁, we have sin θ₂<sin θ₁ ⇒ θ₂<θ₁.

That means the refracted ray bends toward the normal.

Answer: Option 1 – bending towards the normal.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.questionText: Human eye can see objects at different distances with contrasting illuminations. This is due to

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements about Light year:

1. Light year is a unit for measurement of very large distances.

2. Light year is a unit for measurement of very large time intervals.

3. Light year is a unit for measurement of intensity of light.

Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.solution:
Only statement 1 is correct.

A light year is a unit of distance, not time or intensity of light. It is defined as the distance that light travels in a vacuum in one year. The speed of light is approximately $299,792,458$ meters per second, so over the course of a year, this distance is about:

$$ 1 \text{ light year} = 9.461 \times 10^{15} \text{ meters} $$

Thus, the correct option is D: 1 only.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.questionText: The amplitude of sound waves is measured in the units of
questions.solution:
The correct answer is Option A: pressure. Here's why:

Sound waves are longitudinal waves, meaning they travel by compressing and expanding the medium they pass through. The amplitude of a sound wave refers to the maximum displacement of the particles in the medium from their equilibrium position. This displacement directly corresponds to the change in pressure caused by the wave.

Let's break down why the other options are incorrect:

  • Distance: While distance is related to the wavelength of a sound wave (the distance between two consecutive crests or troughs), it doesn't represent the amplitude.
  • Time: Time is related to the frequency of a sound wave (the number of waves passing a point per second), not its amplitude.
  • Speed: The speed of sound depends on the medium it travels through and is not directly related to the amplitude of the wave.

In summary, the amplitude of a sound wave is measured in units of pressure, as it represents the maximum pressure variation caused by the wave.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.questionText: The magnetic field produced by a current-carrying straight wire at a point outside the wire depends
options: inversely on the distance from it
directly on the distance from it
inversely at short distances and directly at large distances from it
directly on the distance (at short distances) and inversely on the distance (at long distances) from it

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.questionText: Which one of the following sketches correctly describes a lever of second class?
questions.solution:
CONCEPT:

Lever of Second Class

A lever of the second class has three main parts:

  • The fulcrum is at one end. The fulcrum is the point where the lever pivots or turns.
  • The load (the thing you want to move or lift) is in the middle.
  • The effort (where you push or pull) is at the other end, opposite the fulcrum.

In a second-class lever:

  • The load is always between the fulcrum and where you apply effort.
  • The arm where you apply effort (effort arm) is always longer than the part holding the load (load arm), so you get a mechanical advantage greater than 1. This means it's easier to lift something.
  • Examples of second-class levers are wheelbarrows, nutcrackers, and bottle openers.

EXPLANATION:

When you look at sketches of different levers:

  • A second-class lever should always have:
    • Fulcrum at one end,
    • Load in the middle,
    • And effort at the other end.
  • The correct sketch must show the load between the fulcrum and the effort.

So, the sketch that correctly shows a lever of the second class is Option 2.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.questionText: Shown in the figure are two plane mirrors XY and YZ (XY ⊥ YZ) joined at their edge. Also shown is a light ray falling on one of the mirrors and reflected back parallel to its original path as a result of this arrangement. The two mirrors are now rotated by an angle θ to their new position X'YZ', as shown. As a result the new reflected ray is at an angle α from the original reflected ray. Then :
questions.solution:
Explanation:

When two plane mirrors are at right angles (90°), a light ray falling on one mirror reflects back in exactly the opposite direction.

This makes the angle between the incoming ray and the outgoing ray equal to 180°.

In the figure, mirrors XY and YZ meet at a right angle.

Now the pair of mirrors is rotated by an angle θ to the new position X'YZ'.

After this rotation, the reflected ray shifts by an angle α compared to the original reflected ray.

However, the reflected ray remains parallel to its original direction, so α = 0°.

Therefore, the new reflected ray is parallel to the original reflected ray.

Additional Information

If two mirrors are placed at an angle other than 90°, the incoming ray and the reflected ray are no longer exactly opposite.

In that case, the angles of the reflected rays depend on the angle between the mirrors and on the incidence and reflection angles at each mirror.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.questionText: Which one of the following statements is correct ?
options: A neutron is formed by combination of an electron and a proton. Therefore, it is neutral
The mass of an electron is about 1/2000 times that of a proton
An isotope of cobalt is used in the treatment of goiter
J. J. Thomson proposed that the nucleus of an atom contains only neutrons
questions.solution:
Option 2 is correct.

Explanation:

The mass of an electron is

$$m_e \approx 9.11\times10^{-31}\,\text{kg},$$

while that of a proton is

$$m_p \approx 1.67\times10^{-27}\,\text{kg}.$$

Their ratio is

$$\frac{m_e}{m_p}\approx\frac{1}{1836}\approx\frac{1}{2000}.$$

Why the others are wrong:

Option 1 describes an outdated idea; neutrons aren’t simply proton+electron combinations.

Option 3 mixes up treatments: radioiodine isotopes help treat goiter, not cobalt.

Option 4 is incorrect—J. J. Thomson’s model didn’t place neutrons in the nucleus (neutrons were discovered later by Chadwick).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.questionText: Which one of the following statements for second law of motion is NOT correct ?
options: The net force on a body is proportional to that body’s acceleration
The net force on a body is in the same direction as the acceleration
The time rate of change of momentum is equal to force
The net force on a body is proportional to that body’s momentum
questions.solution:
The statement which is NOT correct is:

$$\boxed{\text{Option 4: The net force on a body is proportional to that body’s momentum}}$$

Step-by-step:

According to Newton’s Second Law of Motion:

$$F = \frac{dp}{dt}$$

This means:

  • Force is equal to the time rate of change of momentum

  • If mass is constant, then:

$$F = ma$$

So:

  1. Option 1 is correct because force is proportional to acceleration.

  2. Option 2 is correct because acceleration is in the same direction as net force.

  3. Option 3 is correct because it is the actual statement of the second law.

  4. Option 4 is incorrect because force is not proportional to momentum, but to the rate of change of momentum.

Therefore, the correct answer is:

$$\boxed{\text{Option 4}}$$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.questionText: The temperature of a body increases  from 310 K to 340 K. The temperature increase in degree Celsius is
questions.solution:
Temperature difference in kelvin and degree Celsius is the same.

Given:

$$ T_1 = 310\ \text{K}, \quad T_2 = 340\ \text{K} $$

Increase in temperature:

$$ \Delta T = T_2 - T_1 = 340 - 310 = 30\ \text{K} $$

So, the increase in degree Celsius is also:

$$ 30^\circ \text{C} $$

Correct option: 2. 30°C

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.questionText: If the current through an electrical machine running on direct current is $15 A$ and the machine runs for $10$ minutes, the charge that passes through the machine during this time is:
questions.solution:
To determine the charge that passes through the machine, we use the relationship between current, charge, and time. The formula that relates these quantities is given by :

$$ Q = I \times t $$

where :

$$ Q $$ is the charge (in coulombs, C)

$$ I $$ is the current (in amperes, A)

$$ t $$ is the time (in seconds, s)

We are given :

Current, $$ I = 15 \, \text{A} $$

Time, $$ t = 10 \, \text{minutes} $$

First, we need to convert the time from minutes to seconds :

$$ 10 \, \text{minutes} \times 60 \, \text{seconds per minute} = 600 \, \text{seconds} $$

Now, substituting the given values into the formula, we get :

$$ Q = 15 \, \text{A} \times 600 \, \text{s} $$

$$ Q = 9000 \, \text{C} $$

Therefore, the charge that passes through the machine during this time is :

Option D : $$ 9000 \, \text{C} $$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.questionText: The image we see in plane mirror is
options: real and thus can be photographed.
virtual and nearer than the object.
questions.solution:
The image observed in a plane mirror is described as virtual and is laterally inverted. Therefore, the correct option is:

Option C

Virtual and is laterally inverted.

Explanation:

Virtual Image: A virtual image is formed when the outgoing rays from a point of the object appear to diverge. In the case of a plane mirror, the image cannot be projected onto a screen because the light rays do not actually converge at the image location; they only appear to do so when seen by the observer.

Lateral Inversion: This term refers to the horizontal reversal of the image. For example, if the left hand of a person is raised while standing in front of the mirror, it appears that the right hand is raised in the mirror image.

Virtual images cannot be captured directly on a screen but can be seen by looking into the mirror. It is important to note that a photograph of the mirror itself capturing the virtual image can be taken, but it would not be a direct image of the virtual image.
